title: Yorkshire parkin
subtitle: The dark, sticky oat and treacle ginger cake of bonfire night
excerpt: A dense, deeply spiced cake made with oatmeal, black treacle, and ground ginger, baked in a square tin. It is almost too firm and crumbly when first cut; left two days in a tin it becomes glossy, sticky, and completely different.
sourceNotes: Yorkshire parkin is documented in English culinary literature from the seventeenth century. Florence White, Good Things in England (1932), Project Gutenberg, records the parkin tradition of Yorkshire and Lancashire and distinguishes Yorkshire parkin (oatmeal-heavy) from Lancashire parkin (more flour-based). The association with Bonfire Night (5th November) is longstanding. Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136, includes a ginger parkin recipe consistent with the Yorkshire tradition.

content.text: Parkin is a cake that punishes impatience. Cut on the day it is baked, it is crumbly and coarse, the oatmeal still assertive, the ginger still sharp. Left for two days in an airtight tin, a transformation happens: the treacle pulls moisture from the air, the oatmeal softens, and the whole cake becomes sticky and dense and deeply good. This
is not optional. It is the point of the recipe. You cannot rush a parkin.

It is a bonfire night cake in Yorkshire, traditionally eaten on the 5th of November. The combination of black treacle, rolled oats, ginger, and warm spice makes it one of the more distinctively flavoured things in British baking. The method is simple: melt the butter, treacle, and syrup together, then stir everything in. No creaming, no whisking.

attrs.prepNote: medium oatmeal or fine-ground rolled oats

content.text: Heat the oven to 150°C fan (170°C conventional). Line a 20 cm square tin with baking paper. Combine the dry ingredients: {{plain-flour}}, {{rolled-oats}}, {{dark-brown-sugar}}, {{ginger-ground}}, {{mixed-spice}}, and {{bicarbonate-of-soda}} in a large bowl. Melt {{unsalted-butter}}, {{black-treacle}}, and {{golden-syrup}} together in a saucepan over low heat, stirring until smooth. Remove from the heat and stir in {{whole-milk}}. Pour the warm treacle mixture into the dry ingredients, add {{eggs}}, and stir until everything is evenly combined and no dry pockets remain. The batter will be loose and dark.

Pour the batter into the tin. Bake for 50 to 55 minutes until the surface is set and the centre is firm when pressed. A skewer inserted in the middle should come out with a few moist crumbs but no liquid batter. The parkin will shrink slightly from the sides of the tin. Cool completely in the tin, then lift out on the paper. Wrap tightly in a fresh sheet of baking paper, then in foil, and leave in an airtight tin for at least two days before cutting.
Medium oatmeal gives the most authentic texture. Fine rolled oats produce a smoother crumb; jumbo oats make the finished parkin coarser and chewier.
The parkin will keep for three weeks in a tin and continues to improve for the first week.
Parkin that looks underdone when you take it out of the oven often is not: the oatmeal holds a lot of heat and the centre continues to set as it cools. Check with a skewer rather than by appearance.
